content.title: Green roofs
content.summary: {'A': '越来越多的私营公司和城市当局投资于绿色屋顶，以节省能源成本、减轻洪水风险、为城市野生动物提供栖息地、解决空气污染甚至种植食物等多种好处，这些越来越激进的城市设计可以帮助城市适应面临的重大问题，如资源获取和由于城市发展导致的绿地不足。', 'B': '研究表明，城市中的绿色屋顶可以与“活墙”相结合，这些环保墙壁部分或完全被绿植覆盖，包括土壤或水等生长介质。研究还表明，绿色屋顶可以与地面上的排水系统（如街道树木）相结合，以更好地管理水资源，使建筑环境更可持续。此外，还有证据表明绿色屋顶具有社会价值，医生越来越多地为焦虑和抑郁症患者开处方，让他们在户外花园中度过时间。研究发现，即使是最基本的绿色空间也可以提供更好的生活质量给痴呆症患者，并帮助人们避免肥胖。', 'C': '北美洲的绿色屋顶已经成为主流，建筑物上安装了各种广阔、可进入和食品生产的屋顶。城市领导和当局的推动也使得这一运动得以发展，最近，美国旧金山制定了一项政策，要求新建筑物必须有绿色屋顶。加拿大多伦多自上世纪90年代起就有鼓励在屋顶上发展城市农场的政策。这些国家还受益于拥有比世界上许多地方更新的建筑物，这使得安装绿色屋顶更容易。能够在屋顶高度保持足够的水分并将其均匀分布在整个屋顶上对于维持任何绿色屋顶上的植物至关重要，尤其是在“可食用屋顶”上种植水果和蔬菜。而在新建筑物中，这样做要容易得多，因为它们通常能承受更大的重量，而不是对旧建筑进行改造。拥有更坚固的屋顶也使得种植更多种类的植物更容易，因为土壤可以更深。', 'D': '为了使绿色屋顶成为新建筑的常态，需要公共机构和私人投资者的支持，建筑维护人员可能需要获得新的技能，还可能需要志愿者的帮助，同时还需要考虑安装排水路径、满足健康和安全要求、允许公众进入，以及规划限制和安装期间的常规活动干扰等问题。为了说服投资者和开发商安装绿色屋顶是值得的，经济论据仍然是最重要的，自然资本这个术语被发展出来来解释自然的经济价值，例如通过安装自然解决方案来节省因洪水损害、适应气候变化或帮助人们过上更健康更幸福的生活而节省的资金。', 'E': '绿色屋顶的专业知识不断增长，官方标准的制定确保了其正确设计、施工和维护，并能够良好运作；科学技术的进步也导致了概念的新变化，如“蓝色屋顶”和绿色屋顶与太阳能板的结合等；绿色屋顶有潜力改变城市并帮助其可持续发展，需要在其他地方进行研究和复制，以使绿色、蓝色、棕色和食品生产屋顶成为世界各地城市的常态。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Rooftops covered with grass, vegetable gardens and lush foliage are now a common sight in many cities around the world. More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. These increasingly radical urban designs can help cities adapt to the monumental problems they face, such as access to resources and a lack of green space due to development. But the involvement of city authorities, businesses and other institutions is crucial to ensuring their success – as is research investigating different options to suit the variety of rooftop spaces found in cities. The UK is relatively new to developing green roofs, and local governments and institutions are playing a major role in spreading the practice. London is home to much of the UK's green roof market, mainly due to forward-thinking policies such as the London Plan, which has paved the way to more than doubling the area of green roofs in the capital.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Ongoing research is showcasing how green roofs in cities can integrate with 'living walls': environmentally friendly walls which are partially or completely covered with greenery, including a growing medium, such as soil or water. Research also indicates that green roofs can be integrated with drainage systems on the ground, such as street trees, so that the water is managed better and the built environment is made more sustainable. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. And research has found that access to even the most basic green spaces can provide a better quality of life for dementia sufferers and help people avoid obesity.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: In North America, green roofs have become mainstream, with a wide array of expansive, accessible and food-producing roofs installed in buildings. Again, city leaders and authorities have helped push the movement forward – only recently, San Francisco, USA, created a policy requiring new buildings to have green roofs. Toronto, Canada, has policies dating from the 1990s, encouraging the development of urban farms on rooftops. These countries also benefit from having newer buildings than in many parts of the world, which makes it easier to install green roofs. Being able to keep enough water at roof height and distribute it right across the rooftop is crucial to maintaining the plants on any green roof – especially on 'edible roofs' where fruit and vegetables are farmed. And it's much easier to do this in newer buildings, which can typically hold greater weight, than to retro-fit old ones. Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: For green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. Those responsible for maintaining buildings may have to acquire new skills, such as landscaping, and in some cases, volunteers may be needed to help out. Other considerations include installing drainage paths, meeting health and safety requirements and perhaps allowing access for the public, as well as planning restrictions and disruption from regular activities in and around the buildings during installation. To convince investors and developers that installing green roofs is worthwhile, economic arguments are still the most important. The term 'natural capital' has been developed to explain the economic value of nature; for example, measuring the money saved by installing natural solutions to protect against flood damage, adapt to climate change or help people lead healthier and happier lives.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: As the expertise about green roofs grows, official standards have been developed to ensure that they are designed, constructed and maintained properly, and function well. Improvements in the science and technology underpinning green roof development have also led to new variations in the concept. For example, 'blue roofs' enable buildings to hold water over longer periods of time, rather than draining it away quickly – crucial in times of heavier rainfall. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.

Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.

list.text: to provide habitats for a wide range of species

to regulate the temperature of the immediate environment
to generate power from a sustainable source

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Over the past century, a powerful idea has taken root in the educational landscape. The concept of intelligence as something innate has been supplanted by the idea that intelligence is not fixed, and that, with the right training, we can be the authors of our own cognitive capabilities. Psychologist Alfred Binet, the developer of the first intelligence tests, was one of many 19th-century scientists who held that earlier view and sought to quantify cognitive ability. Then, in the early 20th century, progressive thinkers revolted against the notion that inherent ability is destiny. Instead, educators such as John Dewey argued that every child's intelligence could be developed, given the right environment.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: 'Growth mindset theory' is a relatively new – and extremely popular – version of this idea. In many schools today you will see hallways covered in motivational posters and hear speeches on the mindset of great sporting heroes who simply believed their way to the top. A major focus of the growth mindset in schools is coaxing students away from seeing failure as an indication of their ability, and towards seeing it as a chance to improve that ability. As educationalist Jeff Howard noted several decades ago: 'Smart is not something that you just are, smart is something that you can get.'

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: The idea of the growth mindset is based on the work of psychologist Carol Dweck in California in the 1990s. In one key experiment, Dweck divided a group of 10- to 12-year-olds into two groups. All were told that they had achieved a high score on a test but the first group were praised for their intelligence in achieving this, while the others were praised for their effort. The second group – those who had been instilled with a 'growth mindset' – were subsequently far more likely to put effort into future tasks. Meanwhile, the former took on only those tasks that would not risk their sense of worth. This group had inferred that success or failure is due to innate ability, and this 'fixed mindset' had led them to fear of failure and lack of effort. Praising ability actually made the students perform worse, while praising effort emphasised that change was possible.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: One of the greatest impediments to successfully implementing a growth mindset, however, is the education system itself: in many parts of the world, the school climate is obsessed with performance in the form of constant testing, analysing and ranking of students – a key characteristic of the fixed mindset. Nor is it unusual for schools to create a certain cognitive dissonance, when they applaud the benefits of a growth mindset but then hand out fixed target grades in lessons based on performance.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Aside from the implementation problem, the original growth mindset research has also received harsh criticism. The statistician Andrew Gelman claims that 'their research designs have enough degrees of freedom that they could take their data to support just about any theory at all'. Professor of Psychology Timothy Bates, who has been trying to replicate Dweck's work, is finding that the results are repeatedly null. He notes that: 'People with a growth mindset don't cope any better with failure … Kids with the growth mindset aren't getting better grades, either before or after our intervention study.'

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Much of this criticism is not lost on Dweck, and she deserves great credit for responding to it and adapting her work accordingly. In fact, she argues that her work has been misunderstood and misapplied in a range of ways. She has also expressed concerns that her theories are being misappropriated in schools by being conflated with the self-esteem movement: 'For me the growth mindset is a tool for learning and improvement. It's not just a vehicle for making children feel good.'

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: But there is another factor at work here. The failure to translate the growth mindset into the classroom might reflect a misunderstanding of the nature of teaching and learning itself. Growth mindset supporters David Yeager and Gregory Walton claim that interventions should be delivered in a subtle way to maximise their effectiveness. They say that if adolescents perceive a teacher's intervention as conveying that they are in need of help, this could undo its intended effects.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: A lot of what drives students is their innate beliefs and how they perceive themselves. There is a strong correlation between self-perception and achievement, but there is evidence to suggest that the actual effect of achievement on self-perception is stronger than the other way round. To stand up in a classroom and successfully deliver a good speech is a genuine achievement, and that is likely to be more powerfully motivating than vague notions of 'motivation' itself.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: Recent evidence would suggest that growth mindset interventions are not the elixir of student learning that its proponents claim it to be. The growth mindset appears to be a viable construct in the lab, which, when administered in the classroom via targeted interventions, doesn't seem to work. It is hard to dispute that having faith in the capacity to change is a good attribute for students. Paradoxically, however, that aspiration is not well served by direct interventions that try to instil it.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: Motivational posters and talks are often a waste of time, and might well give students a deluded notion of what success actually means. Teaching concrete skills such as how to write an effective introduction to an essay then praising students' effort in getting there is probably a far better way of improving confidence than telling them how unique they are, or indeed how capable they are of changing their own brains. Perhaps growth mindset works best as a philosophy and not an intervention.

content.title: Alfred Wegener: science, exploration and the theory of continental drift

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text:
Introduction
This is a book about the life and scientific work of Alfred Wegener, whose reputation today rests with his theory of continental displacements, better known as 'continental drift'. Wegener proposed this theory in 1912 and developed it extensively for nearly 20 years. His book on the subject, The Origin of Continents and Oceans, went through four editions and was the focus of an international controversy in his lifetime and for some years after his death.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Wegener's basic idea was that many mysteries about the Earth's history could be solved if one supposed that the continents moved laterally, rather than supposing that they remained fixed in place. Wegener showed in great detail how such continental movements were plausible and how they worked, using evidence from a large number of sciences including geology, geophysics, paleontology, and climatology. Wegener's idea – that the continents move – is at the heart of the theory that guides Earth sciences today: namely plate tectonics. Plate tectonics is in many respects quite different from Wegener's proposal, in the same way that modern evolutionary theory is very different from the ideas Charles Darwin proposed in the 1850s about biological evolution. Yet plate tectonics is a descendant of Alfred Wegener's theory of continental drift, in quite the same way that modern evolutionary theory is a descendant of Darwin's theory of natural selection.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: When I started writing about Wegener's life and work, one of the most intriguing things about him for me was that, although he came up with a theory on continental drift, he was not a geologist. He trained as an astronomer and pursued a career in atmospheric physics. When he proposed the theory of continental displacements in 1912, he was a lecturer in physics and astronomy at the University of Marburg, in southern Germany. However, he was not an 'unknown'. In 1906 he had set a world record (with his brother Kurt) for time aloft in a hot-air balloon: 52 hours. Between 1906 and 1908 he had taken part in a highly publicized and extremely dangerous expedition to the coast of northeast Greenland. He had also made a name for himself amongst a small circle of meteorologists and atmospheric physicists in Germany as the author of a textbook, Thermodynamics of the Atmosphere (1911), and of a number of interesting scientific papers.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: As important as Wegener's work on continental drift has turned out to be, it was largely a sideline to his interest in atmospheric physics, geophysics, and paleoclimatology, and thus I have been at great pains to put Wegener's work on continental drift in the larger context of his other scientific work, and in the even larger context of atmospheric sciences in his lifetime. This is a 'continental drift book' only to the extent that Wegener was interested in that topic and later became famous for it. My treatment of his other scientific work is no less detailed, though I certainly have devoted more attention to the reception of his ideas on continental displacement, as they were much more controversial than his other work.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Readers interested in the specific detail of Wegener's career will see that he often stopped pursuing a given line of investigation (sometimes for years on end), only to pick it up later. I have tried to provide guideposts to his rapidly shifting interests by characterizing different phases of his life as careers in different sciences, which is reflected in the titles of the chapters. Thus, the index should be a sufficient guide for those interested in a particular aspect of Wegener's life but perhaps not all of it. My own feeling, however, is that the parts do not make as much sense on their own as do all of his activities taken together. In this respect I urge readers to try to experience Wegener's life as he lived it, with all the interruptions, changes of mind, and renewed efforts this entailed.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Wegener left behind a few published works but, as was standard practice, these reported the results of his work – not the journey he took to reach that point. Only a few hundred of the many thousands of letters he wrote and received in his lifetime have survived and he didn't keep notebooks or diaries that recorded his life and activities. He was not active (with a few exceptions) in scientific societies, and did not seek to find influence or advance his ideas through professional contacts and politics, spending most of his time at home in his study reading and writing, or in the field collecting observations.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Some famous scientists, such as Newton, Darwin, and Einstein, left mountains of written material behind, hundreds of notebooks and letters numbering in the tens of thousands. Others, like Michael Faraday, left extensive journals of their thoughts and speculations, parallel to their scientific notebooks. The more such material a scientist leaves behind, the better chance a biographer has of forming an accurate picture of how a scientist's ideas took shape and evolved.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: I am firmly of the opinion that most of us, Wegener included, are not in any real sense the authors of our own lives. We plan, think, and act, often with apparent freedom, but most of the time our lives 'happen to us', and we only retrospectively turn this happenstance into a coherent narrative of fulfilled intentions. This book, therefore, is a story both of the life and scientific work that Alfred Wegener planned and intended and of the life and scientific work that actually 'happened to him'. These are, as I think you will soon see, not always the same thing.

groups.desc:
Questions 27–30

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in Reading Passage 3?
Choose
YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this

questions.title: Wegener's ideas about continental drift were widely disputed while he was alive.
questions.analysis: 题干中的 "Wegener's ideas about continental drift" 对应原文中的 "his theory of continental displacements, better known as 'continental drift'"，"were widely disputed" 对应 "was the focus of an international controversy"，"while he was alive" 对应 "in his lifetime"。这些同义替换词在原文中明确指出了魏格纳的大陆漂移理论在他生前引起了广泛的争议，因此答案是 YES。

central_sentences.sentence: His book on the subject, The Origin of Continents and Oceans, went through four editions and was the focus of an international controversy in his lifetime and for some years after his death.
central_sentences.translation: 他关于这个主题的著作《大陆与海洋的起源》出版了四个版本，在他的一生中以及死后的几年里都是国际争议的焦点。

target_sen.target_sen: This is a book about the life and scientific work of Alfred Wegener, whose reputation today rests with his theory of continental displacements, better known as 'continental drift'.
This is a book about the life and scientific work of Alfred Wegener, whose reputation today rests with his theory of continental displacements, better known as 'continental drift'.
His book on the subject, The Origin of Continents and Oceans, went through four editions and was the focus of an international controversy in his lifetime and for some years after his death.
His book on the subject, The Origin of Continents and Oceans, went through four editions and was the focus of an international controversy in his lifetime and for some years after his death.

questions.title: The idea that the continents remained fixed in place was defended in a number of respected scientific publications.
questions.analysis: 题干中的线索词“continents remained fixed in place”和“respected scientific publications”在原文中没有明确的同义替换词。虽然文章提到魏格纳的理论引起了国际争议，但并没有具体提到反对大陆漂移理论的受尊敬的科学出版物。因此，题干中的信息在文章中找不到，正确答案是NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: This is a 'continental drift book' only to the extent that Wegener was interested in that topic and later became famous for it.
central_sentences.translation: 这是一本"大陆漂移"的书，仅限于魏格纳对这个主题感兴趣并后来因此而出名的程度。

questions.title: Wegener relied on a limited range of scientific fields to support his theory of continental drift.
questions.analysis: 题干中的 "relied on a limited range of scientific fields" 意思是魏格纳依赖于有限的科学领域来支持他的大陆漂移理论。而原文中提到 "using evidence from a large number of sciences" 表明魏格纳使用了大量科学领域的证据，这与题干中的 "limited range" 是相反的。因此，答案是 NO。

central_sentences.sentence: Wegener showed in great detail how such continental movements were plausible and how they worked, using evidence from a large number of sciences including geology, geophysics, paleontology, and climatology.
central_sentences.translation: 韦格纳详细展示了这种大陆运动如何是合理的，以及它们是如何运作的，他使用了来自大量科学领域的证据，包括地质学、地球物理学、古生物学和气候学。

target_sen.target_sen: Wegener showed in great detail how such continental movements were plausible and how they worked, using evidence from a large number of sciences including geology, geophysics, paleontology, and climatology.

Wegener showed in great detail how such continental movements were plausible and how they worked, using evidence from a large number of sciences including geology, geophysics, paleontology, and climatology.
Wegener's idea – that the continents move – is at the heart of the theory that guides Earth sciences today: namely plate tectonics.

questions.title: The similarities between Wegener's theory of continental drift and modern-day plate tectonics are enormous.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到韦格纳的大陆漂移理论与现代板块构造理论之间的相似性非常大，而原文明确指出两者在许多方面有很大不同。因此，题干的陈述与原文信息相反，正确答案是NO。

central_sentences.sentence: Plate tectonics is in many respects quite different from Wegener's proposal, in the same way that modern evolutionary theory is very different from the ideas Charles Darwin proposed in the 1850s about biological evolution.
central_sentences.translation: 板块构造理论在许多方面与韦格纳的理论有很大不同，就像现代进化理论与达尔文在19世纪50年代提出的生物进化理论有很大不同一样。

target_sen.target_sen: Plate tectonics is in many respects quite different from Wegener's proposal, in the same way that modern evolutionary theory is very different from the ideas Charles Darwin proposed in the 1850s about biological evolution.

Plate tectonics is in many respects quite different from Wegener's proposal, in the same way that modern evolutionary theory is very different from the ideas Charles Darwin proposed in the 1850s about biological evolution.
Wegener's idea – that the continents move – is at the heart of the theory that guides Earth sciences today: namely plate tectonics.
Yet plate tectonics is a descendant of Alfred Wegener's theory of continental drift, in quite the same way that modern evolutionary theory is a descendant of Darwin's theory of natural selection.

groups.title: Wegener's life and work
groups.desc:
Questions 31–36

Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A–J, below
groups.collect: One of the remarkable things about Wegener from a$151$ is that although he proposed a theory of continental drift, he was not a geologist. His $152$were limited to atmospheric physics. However, at the time he proposed his theory of continental drift in 1912, he was already a person of $153$. Six years previously, there had been his $154$of 52 hours in a hot-air balloon, followed by his well-publicised but $155$of Greenland's coast. With the publication of his textbook on thermodynamics, he had also come to the attention of a$156$ of German scientists.

questions.analysis: 正确答案是 "biographer's perspective"。题干中的 "remarkable things" 对应文章中的 "intriguing things"，而 "from a" 对应 "for me"，这表明这是作者的视角，即传记作者的视角。

central_sentences.sentence: When I started writing about Wegener's life and work, one of the most intriguing things about him for me was that, although he came up with a theory on continental drift, he was not a geologist.
central_sentences.translation: 当我开始撰写韦格纳的生平和工作时，对我来说，关于他最有趣的事情之一是，尽管他提出了大陆漂移理论，但他并不是一名地质学家。

target_sen.target_sen: When I started writing about Wegener's life and work, one of the most intriguing things about him for me was that, although he came up with a theory on continental drift, he was not a geologist.
When I started writing about Wegener's life and work, one of the most intriguing things about him for me was that, although he came up with a theory on continental drift, he was not a geologist.

questions.analysis: 题干问的是魏格纳的什么东西限于大气物理学。原文中提到他的大陆漂移工作是他对大气物理学、地球物理学和古气候学兴趣的副业。这里的 "interest in atmospheric physics" 就是职业兴趣。因此，最直接的对应关系是选项 F: professional interests（职业兴趣）。

central_sentences.sentence: As important as Wegener's work on continental drift has turned out to be, it was largely a sideline to his interest in atmospheric physics, geophysics, and paleoclimatology, and thus I have been at great pains to put Wegener's work on continental drift in the larger context of his other scientific work, and in the even larger context of atmospheric sciences in his lifetime.
central_sentences.translation: 尽管魏格纳在大陆漂移方面的工作最终被证明非常重要，但这主要是他对大气物理学、地球物理学和古气候学兴趣的附带产物。因此，我一直努力将魏格纳在大陆漂移方面的工作置于他其他科学工作的更大背景中，以及他一生中大气科学的更广泛背景中。

target_sen.target_sen: As important as Wegener's work on continental drift has turned out to be, it was largely a sideline to his interest in atmospheric physics, geophysics, and paleoclimatology, and thus I have been at great pains to put Wegener's work on continental drift in the larger context of his other scientific work, and in the even larger context of atmospheric sciences in his lifetime.

As important as Wegener's work on continental drift has turned out to be, it was largely a sideline to his interest in atmospheric physics, geophysics, and paleoclimatology, and thus I have been at great pains to put Wegener's work on continental drift in the larger context of his other scientific work, and in the even larger context of atmospheric sciences in his lifetime.

questions.analysis: 题干中的“proposed his theory of continental drift in 1912”在原文中对应“proposed the theory of continental displacements in 1912”，而“person of”对应“not an 'unknown'”。这表明Wegener在1912年提出大陆漂移理论时已经有了一定的名气。选项A“modest fame”最符合这个描述，因此是正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: However, he was not an 'unknown'.
central_sentences.translation: 然而，他并不是一个无名之辈。

syn_ary.synonym: proposed the theory of continental displacements in 1912

target_sen.target_sen: When he proposed the theory of continental displacements in 1912, he was a lecturer in physics and astronomy at the University of Marburg, in southern Germany.

locating_words.locating_word: proposed his theory of continental drift in 1912

questions.analysis: 题干中的“52 hours in a hot-air balloon”直接对应文章中的“52 hours in a hot-air balloon”。文章中提到“set a world record”，这与选项中的“record-breaking achievement”是同义表达。因此，正确答案是“record-breaking achievement”。

central_sentences.sentence: In 1906 he had set a world record (with his brother Kurt) for time aloft in a hot-air balloon: 52 hours.
central_sentences.translation: 1906年，他和他的兄弟Kurt一起创造了热气球飞行时间的世界纪录：52小时。

questions.analysis: 正确答案是“hazardous exploration”，因为它最直接对应了文章中的“highly publicized and extremely dangerous expedition”。通过关键词对比和排除法，即使词汇量有限的学生也能做对这道题。

central_sentences.sentence: Between 1906 and 1908 he had taken part in a highly publicized and extremely dangerous expedition to the coast of northeast Greenland.
central_sentences.translation: 在1906年至1908年间，他参加了一次备受瞩目且极其危险的格陵兰岛东北海岸探险活动。

target_sen.target_sen: Between 1906 and 1908 he had taken part in a highly publicized and extremely dangerous expedition to the coast of northeast Greenland.
Between 1906 and 1908 he had taken part in a highly publicized and extremely dangerous expedition to the coast of northeast Greenland.

questions.analysis: 题干中的 "come to the attention of" 对应原文中的 "made a name for himself amongst"，而 "German scientists" 对应 "meteorologists and atmospheric physicists in Germany"。原文中提到 "a small circle"（一小圈）最接近的选项是 "select group"（选择的群体），因此正确答案是 "select group"。

central_sentences.sentence: He had also made a name for himself amongst a small circle of meteorologists and atmospheric physicists in Germany as the author of a textbook, Thermodynamics of the Atmosphere (1911), and of a number of interesting scientific papers.
central_sentences.translation: 作为《大气热力学》（1911年）教科书的作者，以及一些有趣的科学论文的作者，他在德国气象学家和大气物理学家的小圈子里也赢得了名声。

target_sen.target_sen: He had also made a name for himself amongst a small circle of meteorologists and atmospheric physicists in Germany as the author of a textbook, Thermodynamics of the Atmosphere (1911), and of a number of interesting scientific papers.

groups.desc:
Questions 37–40

Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.title: What is Mott T Greene doing in the fifth paragraph?

options.text: describing what motivated him to write the book
explaining why it is desirable to read the whole book
suggesting why Wegener pursued so many different careers
indicating what aspects of Wegener's life interested him most

questions.analysis: 在这段话中，作者Mott T Greene解释了为什么通读整本书是值得的。他认为，魏格纳的生活和工作经历是一个整体，各部分单独看不如整体连贯来看更有意义。因此，他建议读者通读整本书，以全面了解魏格纳的生活和工作。这正是选项B所表达的内容。

central_sentences.sentence: My own feeling, however, is that the parts do not make as much sense on their own as do all of his activities taken together. In this respect I urge readers to try to experience Wegener's life as he lived it, with all the interruptions, changes of mind, and renewed efforts this entailed.
central_sentences.translation: 然而，我个人的感觉是，单独的部分并不如把他所有的活动放在一起那样有意义。因此，我建议读者尝试去体验魏格纳的人生，就像他自己经历的那样，包括所有的中断、想法的改变和重新付出的努力。

questions.title: What is said about Wegener in the sixth paragraph?

options.text: He was not a particularly ambitious person.
He kept a record of all his scientific observations.
He did not adopt many of the scientific practices of the time.
He enjoyed discussing new discoveries with other scientists.

questions.analysis: 文章中提到Wegener没有通过专业联系和政治来寻求影响力或推进他的想法，这表明他不是一个特别有野心的人。因此，选项A是正确的。

central_sentences.sentence: He was not active (with a few exceptions) in scientific societies, and did not seek to find influence or advance his ideas through professional contacts and politics, spending most of his time at home in his study reading and writing, or in the field collecting observations.
central_sentences.translation: 他在科学社团中不太活跃（只有少数例外），并且没有通过专业联系和政治来寻求影响力或推进他的想法，而是将大部分时间花在家中的书房里阅读和写作，或者在野外收集观察数据。

target_sen.target_sen: Wegener left behind a few published works but, as was standard practice, these reported the results of his work – not the journey he took to reach that point.

questions.title: What does Greene say about some other famous scientists?

options.text: Their published works had a greater impact than Wegener's did.
They had fewer doubts about their scientific ideas than Wegener did.
Their scientific ideas were more controversial than Wegener's.
They are easier subjects to write about than Wegener.

questions.analysis: Greene提到，像Newton、Darwin和Einstein这样的著名科学家留下了大量的书面材料，这使得传记作者更容易形成一个准确的科学思想演变的图景。因此，这些科学家比Wegener更容易写传记。选项D "They are easier subjects to write about than Wegener" 正确地反映了这一点。

central_sentences.sentence: Some famous scientists, such as Newton, Darwin, and Einstein, left mountains of written material behind, hundreds of notebooks and letters numbering in the tens of thousands. Others, like Michael Faraday, left extensive journals of their thoughts and speculations, parallel to their scientific notebooks. The more such material a scientist leaves behind, the better chance a biographer has of forming an accurate picture of how a scientist's ideas took shape and evolved.
central_sentences.translation: 一些著名科学家，如牛顿、达尔文和爱因斯坦，留下了大量的书面材料，包括数百本笔记本和成千上万封信件。其他人，如迈克尔·法拉第，留下了大量记录他们思想和推测的日志，与他们的科学笔记本并列。科学家留下的材料越多，传记作者就越有机会形成一个关于科学家思想如何形成和演变的准确图景。

syn_ary.synonym: Some famous scientists, such as Newton, Darwin, and Einstein

target_sen.target_sen: Some famous scientists, such as Newton, Darwin, and Einstein, left mountains of written material behind, hundreds of notebooks and letters numbering in the tens of thousands.

questions.title: What is Greene's main point in the final paragraph?

options.text: It is not enough in life to have good intentions.
People need to plan carefully if they want to succeed.
People have little control over many aspects of their lives.
It is important that people ensure they have the freedom to act.

questions.analysis: Greene在最后一段的主要观点是人们对自己生活的许多方面几乎没有控制权。文章中提到“我们大多数人，包括韦格纳在内，从某种意义上说都不是自己生活的作者”，以及“我们的生活是‘发生在我们身上’的”，这些都表明了生活中很多事情是我们无法控制的。因此，选项C是正确的。

central_sentences.sentence: I am firmly of the opinion that most of us, Wegener included, are not in any real sense the authors of our own lives.
central_sentences.translation: 我坚信我们大多数人，包括韦格纳在内，在任何真实意义上都不是我们自己生活的作者。

central_sentences.sentence: We plan, think, and act, often with apparent freedom, but most of the time our lives 'happen to us', and we only retrospectively turn this happenstance into a coherent narrative of fulfilled intentions.
central_sentences.translation: 我们计划、思考和行动，经常表现出明显的自由，但大多数时候我们的生活"发生在我们身上"，我们只是事后将这些偶然事件转化为一个连贯的、实现了意图的叙事。
